concerns.text: FR-002 mandates a 'fuzzy match' on elemental weight fractions and environment to merge Materials Project (MP) and NIST data. MP contains DFT-computed ground-state properties for ideal crystal structures, NOT experimental corrosion potentials in specific pH/temperature environments. There is no physical basis to link a static DFT entry to a dynamic electrochemical measurement via fuzzy matching. This creates a fundamental category error: the 'target' variable (corrosion potential) does not exist in the MP source, and the 'predictor' (composition) in MP is not the experimental composition of the corroded sample. The resulting dataset will be scientifically invalid for regression.

The validation strategy (SC-001) relies on R² scores from a model trained on a dataset constructed via the flawed merge in FR-002. If the merge pairs incorrect compositions with environmental conditions (due to fuzzy matching on non-unique alloy names or missing metadata), the model learns noise. A high R² in this context would be a spurious correlation, not evidence of a learnable physical relationship. The validation target (R²) is not independent of the data construction error; it is a direct measure of the noise introduced by the invalid merge.

The 'Alloy Family' definition (primary base metal) for the Leave-One-Alloy-Family-Out split is insufficient to prevent data leakage. Alloys within the same family (e.g., SS304 vs SS316) share highly similar elemental weight fractions. A model trained on SS304 can trivially generalize to SS316 based on shared composition, meaning the test set is not 'unseen' in terms of the predictor space. This violates the independence of the validation target from the predictors, rendering the generalization claim (US-1) scientifically unsound.

FR-008 proposes a 'one-sample permutation test' with only 100 permutations to generate p-values for feature importance. For a dataset of ~500-2000 records, 100 permutations yield a minimum p-value resolution of 0.01. This is statistically underpowered to distinguish true signal from noise, especially after Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons. The resulting p-values will be unstable and unable to support the claim of 'robustness' (SC-003), making the statistical validation methodologically invalid for the claimed scientific inference.

The spec assumes 'Median imputation' for missing pH (US-1, Assumptions). Imputing a critical environmental variable (pH) with a median value destroys the variance required to learn the interaction between composition and environment. If pH is missing, the environmental context is unknown; imputing it assumes an 'average' environment, which biases the model and invalidates the partial dependence plots (US-3) for pH interactions. This introduces a systematic bias that cannot be corrected by the model, rendering the interaction analysis scientifically unsound.

The spec assumes the NIST database contains explicit numerical pH and temperature values. If records only contain qualitative descriptions (e.g., 'acidic'), the system excludes them. This assumption ignores the reality of corrosion data, where environmental conditions are often poorly documented. Relying on this assumption without a robust strategy for handling qualitative data (beyond exclusion) risks a dataset too small to support the claimed analysis, invalidating the research design.
